name: Urea cycle (Krebs–Henseleit ornithine cycle)
summary: Hepatic two-compartment cycle that detoxifies ammonia by condensing it with bicarbonate and aspartate into urea for renal excretion, regenerating ornithine on every turn.
description: The urea cycle is the mammalian route for disposing of the nitrogen released by amino-acid catabolism. It runs almost exclusively in periportal hepatocytes and spans two compartments. The first nitrogen enters in the **mitochondrial matrix**: carbamoyl-phosphate synthetase I (CPS1) fixes ammonia and bicarbonate into carbamoyl phosphate at the cost of 2 ATP, and ornithine transcarbamylase (OTC) transfers the carbamoyl group onto ornithine to make citrulline. Citrulline is exported to the **cytosol** by the ornithine/citrulline antiporter ORNT1 (SLC25A15). There the second nitrogen is donated by aspartate: argininosuccinate synthase (ASS1) ligates citrulline and aspartate (consuming ATP → AMP + PPi), argininosuccinate lyase (ASL) cleaves the product into arginine and fumarate, and arginase-1 (ARG1) hydrolyzes arginine to urea plus ornithine, which re-enters the mitochondrion to close the cycle. Net cost is four high-energy phosphate bonds per urea (3 ATP → 2 ADP + AMP). CPS1 is the committed, rate-limiting step and is catalytically inert without its essential allosteric activator **N-acetylglutamate (NAG)**, whose synthesis by NAGS is in turn stimulated by arginine — coupling flux to nitrogen load. The fumarate released by ASL and the aspartate consumed by ASS1 constitute the **aspartate–argininosuccinate shunt** that ties the urea cycle to the citric-acid cycle (regenerating oxaloacetate → aspartate) and to gluconeogenesis. Long-term capacity is set transcriptionally: glucagon (via cAMP) and glucocorticoids induce all five enzymes on a high-protein diet or during starvation. Inherited defects in each enzyme or transporter cause the urea-cycle disorders, whose shared hallmark is hyperammonemic encephalopathy.

sources.id: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8e, Ch. 18 (Amino Acid Oxidation and the Production of Urea)

references.title: Morris SM Jr. Regulation of enzymes of the urea cycle and arginine metabolism. Annu Rev Nutr.
Suggested guidelines for the diagnosis and management of urea cycle disorders: first revision (Häberle et al.)

compartments.name: Mitochondrial matrix
compartments.description: NAGS, CPS1 and OTC operate here; the first nitrogen is fixed into carbamoyl phosphate.

compartments.name: Mitochondrial inner membrane
compartments.description: ORNT1 (SLC25A15) and citrin (SLC25A13) antiporters shuttle ornithine/citrulline and aspartate/glutamate between matrix and cytosol.

compartments.name: Cytosol
compartments.description: ASS1, ASL and ARG1 operate here; the second nitrogen is added and urea is released.

metabolites.name: Ammonia
metabolites.description: Neurotoxic nitrogenous waste from amino-acid catabolism; the first nitrogen of urea. Predominantly the NH4+ ion at physiological pH.

metabolites.name: Bicarbonate
metabolites.description: Carbon and one oxygen of the urea carbonyl; the physiological carbon substrate of CPS1.

metabolites.name: Diphosphate (PPi)
metabolites.description: Released by ASS1; its subsequent hydrolysis by inorganic pyrophosphatase pulls argininosuccinate synthesis forward.

metabolites.name: Carbamoyl phosphate
metabolites.description: High-energy carrier of the first urea nitrogen, made by CPS1 in the matrix.

metabolites.name: L-Ornithine
metabolites.description: The cycle's regenerating carrier; consumed by OTC and re-formed by ARG1.

metabolites.name: L-Citrulline
metabolites.description: Product of OTC; carries the first nitrogen from mitochondrion to cytosol.

metabolites.name: L-Aspartate
metabolites.description: Donor of the second urea nitrogen; regenerated from citric-acid-cycle oxaloacetate by transamination.

metabolites.name: N-(L-Arginino)succinate
metabolites.description: Transient condensation product of citrulline and aspartate; cleaved by ASL.

metabolites.name: L-Arginine
metabolites.description: Immediate precursor of urea; also a hub metabolite feeding nitric-oxide and creatine synthesis.

metabolites.name: Fumarate
metabolites.description: Co-product of ASL; enters the citric-acid cycle (aspartate–argininosuccinate shunt) and gluconeogenesis.

metabolites.name: Urea
metabolites.description: The excretory end product; carries two nitrogen atoms per molecule to the kidney.

metabolites.name: N-Acetyl-L-glutamate
metabolites.description: Essential allosteric activator of CPS1; its concentration effectively gates urea-cycle flux.

metabolites.name: Acetyl-CoA
metabolites.description: Acetyl donor for NAG synthesis by NAGS.

metabolites.name: L-Glutamate
metabolites.description: Substrate for NAG synthesis and the counter-ion exchanged by citrin; a central hub for hepatic ammonia handling.

metabolites.name: Cortisol
metabolites.description: Representative glucocorticoid; with glucagon it transcriptionally induces urea-cycle enzymes during high nitrogen load.

enzymes.name: N-acetylglutamate synthase, mitochondrial
enzymes.description: Produces the essential CPS1 activator N-acetylglutamate; arginine-stimulated. NAGS deficiency is a treatable urea-cycle disorder (responds to the NAG analog carglumic acid).

enzymes.name: Carbamoyl-phosphate synthetase I (ammonia), mitochondrial
enzymes.description: Committed, rate-limiting step; catalytically inactive without allosteric N-acetylglutamate. CPS1 deficiency causes severe neonatal hyperammonemia.

enzymes.name: Ornithine transcarbamylase (ornithine carbamoyltransferase), mitochondrial
enzymes.description: Transfers the carbamoyl group to ornithine. OTC deficiency is X-linked and the most common urea-cycle disorder.

enzymes.name: Argininosuccinate synthase
enzymes.description: Ligates citrulline and aspartate at the cost of ATP → AMP + PPi. ASS1 deficiency causes citrullinemia type I.

enzymes.name: Argininosuccinate lyase
enzymes.description: Eliminates fumarate to yield arginine. ASL deficiency causes argininosuccinic aciduria.

enzymes.name: Arginase-1 (liver-type)
enzymes.description: Hydrolyzes arginine to urea and ornithine, closing the cycle. ARG1 deficiency causes hyperargininemia with progressive spastic diplegia.

enzymes.name: Mitochondrial ornithine transporter 1 (ORNT1)
enzymes.description: Antiports cytosolic ornithine for mitochondrial citrulline. Loss of function causes HHH syndrome (hyperornithinemia–hyperammonemia–homocitrullinuria).

enzymes.name: Citrin / aspartate-glutamate carrier 2 (AGC2)
enzymes.description: Electrogenic exporter of aspartate (for ASS1) in exchange for glutamate + H+; also part of the malate–aspartate shuttle. Deficiency causes citrullinemia type II (CTLN2/NICCD).

provenance.notes: Not part of the cyclic carbon flux, but produces the obligatory allosteric activator of CPS1.

reactions.name: Carbamoyl phosphate synthesis
reactions.equation: NH3 + HCO3- + 2 ATP -> carbamoyl phosphate + 2 ADP + Pi + H+

provenance.notes: Committed, rate-limiting step; absolutely requires N-acetylglutamate. KEGG R00149 writes the carbon substrate as CO2 + H2O; bicarbonate (HCO3-) is the accepted physiological substrate shown here.
Exports newly made citrulline to the cytosol and imports ornithine for the next OTC turn; defective in HHH syndrome.

reactions.name: Aspartate/glutamate antiport (citrin, AGC2)
reactions.equation: L-aspartate [matrix] + L-glutamate [cytosol] + H+ [cytosol] <=> L-aspartate [cytosol] + L-glutamate [matrix] + H+ [matrix]

provenance.notes: Supplies cytosolic aspartate (the second-nitrogen donor) for ASS1; electrogenic. Defective in citrullinemia type II.

reactions.name: Argininosuccinate synthesis
reactions.equation: L-citrulline + L-aspartate + ATP -> argininosuccinate + AMP + PPi + H+

provenance.notes: Introduces the second nitrogen from aspartate; often cited as the flux-controlling step of the cytosolic segment.
Fumarate links the urea cycle to the citric-acid cycle (aspartate–argininosuccinate shunt).
Regenerates ornithine (returned to the matrix by ORNT1) and releases urea for excretion.

regulations.mechanism: N-acetylglutamate is an obligatory allosteric activator of CPS1; the enzyme is essentially inactive without it, so NAG concentration sets urea-cycle flux.
L-arginine allosterically activates N-acetylglutamate synthase, raising NAG and hence CPS1 activity — a feed-forward signal that ramps up ureagenesis when amino acids/arginine are abundant.
Glucocorticoids (and glucagon acting via cAMP) transcriptionally induce CPS1 and the other urea-cycle enzymes during starvation or a high-protein diet, expanding long-term ureagenic capacity.

sources.id: Morris SM Jr, Annu Rev Nutr 2002;22:87-105

relations.note: Fumarate from ASL enters the TCA cycle (aspartate–argininosuccinate shunt), where it is oxidized to oxaloacetate and transaminated back to aspartate.
Aspartate, the second-nitrogen donor consumed by ASS1, is regenerated from TCA-cycle oxaloacetate by aspartate aminotransferase (GOT2).
Fumarate → malate → oxaloacetate feeds hepatic gluconeogenesis, co-localized with ureagenesis in periportal hepatocytes.
The ammonia entering CPS1 derives largely from hepatic glutamate dehydrogenase and glutaminase acting on glutamate and glutamine.
Ornithine is the committed precursor of the polyamines (putrescine → spermidine → spermine) via ornithine decarboxylase.
Arginine is the substrate of nitric-oxide synthase, which produces NO and regenerates citrulline (the citrulline–NO cycle).
